text:
Thanks for the episode y'all :+1:

IME, AutoHotkey gives "Computer" feeling back to Microsoft Windows

elements.text: IME, AutoHotkey gives "Computer" feeling back to Microsoft Windows

text:
This is great!

After that episode, I dug out an old Snow Leopard-era iMac and put Kid Pix on it. Been having a blast with that — and my 6yo is doing so much more with it than any of the iPad drawing apps we've tried. (As soon as she gets a little further on reading, I'm gonna try learning etoys with her)

So maybe the computer feeling is just nostalgia, because software was truly better back then.

text:
Unless there are already-provable upsides, one should continue to entertain the possibility that there are no upsides.

The time spent fixing type errors before and after pushing to github needs to contrasted with the time spent just tinkering with a design in an iterative manner without appeasing a type checker.

My gut says that the time spent is relatively equal in both cases, i.e. using type-checkers doesn't actually improve eventual productivity over just thinking about a problem.

My gut, further, says that designing with strict type checking results in stunted designs. Type checking is good for Production Engineering, but discourages good Design Engineering. Design Engineering requires iteration to understand a problem domain, something which is hindered by bowing down to the restrictions imposed by type checkers.
